skill.name: azure-identity-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Identity SDK in Python with DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, token retrieval, and async clients.
skill.summary: Build Azure Identity authentication flows for Python Azure SDK clients.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and environment-variable patterns, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples for Azure Identity SDK usage. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or executable helper script was found in SKILL.md. Residual risk is low because the skill discusses credential environment variables and token retrieval that users must handle carefully.

low_findings.title: Credential Handling Guidance Requires Care
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for credential-related content, but low severity in context. SKILL.md documents AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, and os.environ usage for legitimate Azure SDK authentication examples. The file also advises users not to hardcode credentials, so no malicious collection or exfiltration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names and os.environ reads are clearly present, but they appear in documentation examples for Azure authentication. The surrounding best-practice text discourages hardcoded secrets.

low_findings.title: External Command Patterns Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The shell, Azure CLI, Azure Developer CLI, and PowerShell patterns are fenced code blocks or table entries that explain installation and credential chain behavior. No script file, subprocess call, or instruction that executes hidden commands was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged command strings are visible Markdown examples and credential names. There is no executable wrapper or dynamic command construction in the only scanned file.

low_findings.title: Azure Service URLs Are Legitimate Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for suspicious network behavior. The hardcoded URLs are Azure service endpoints and OAuth scope strings used in examples for Blob Storage, Azure Resource Manager, and Azure Database for PostgreSQL. No request sends user data to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are standard Azure placeholder endpoints or resource scopes in SDK examples. I did not find evidence of data exfiltration or non-Azure network targets.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Hidden File Detection Is Scope Syntax
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The scanner appears to classify .default in Azure OAuth scope strings as hidden file access. The affected lines are token scopes passed to credential.get_token, not filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only hidden-file-like token is .default inside Azure OAuth scope strings. There are no file read or write operations in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The import alert maps to a multiline Python import statement for azure.identity classes, not dynamic code loading. The weak cryptography alert maps to frontmatter description text and has no cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows descriptive metadata and a normal Python import block. I did not find eval, importlib, weak hash usage, or encryption code.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristics Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The scanner combined documentation examples for commands, Azure URLs, and credential environment variables into critical heuristics. Review found a single Markdown file with instructional SDK examples and no prompt injection attempt or malicious execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk factors are present as topic content, but semantic review did not find executable code or an exfiltration workflow. The only scanned file is documentation.

content.user_title: Build Azure Identity Auth in Python
content.value_statement: Azure authentication setup is easy to misconfigure across local, CI, and hosted environments. This skill provides focused Python examples for DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, and token usage.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import the Azure Identity SDK for Python.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential and its credential chain order.
Provides examples for managed identity and service principal credentials.
Shows how to request Azure access tokens for specific resource scopes.
Includes async credential usage and cleanup guidance.
limitations: Does not validate Azure tenant, client, or subscription configuration.
Does not create Azure resources or assign managed identity permissions.
Does not replace official Azure security policy or compliance review.
Examples require users to protect secrets and tokens in their own environment.

use_cases.title: Add Azure SDK Authentication
use_cases.description: Use DefaultAzureCredential to connect Python Azure SDK clients across local development and Azure hosting.

use_cases.title: Prepare CI Authentication
use_cases.description: Configure service principal environment variables for automated Python jobs that access Azure services.

use_cases.title: Migrate to Managed Identity
use_cases.description: Replace stored secrets with managed identity patterns for Azure-hosted Python workloads.

prompt_templates.title: Start with DefaultAzureCredential
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an Azure SDK client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use DefaultAzureCredential with a Python Azure SDK client for local development and Azure hosting.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Credential Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, and ClientSecretCredential for my Python workload.

prompt_templates.title: Review Service Principal Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Azure service principal authentication flow for Python and list security checks before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Credential Chain
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ChainedTokenCredential strategy for a Python app that prefers managed identity and supports secure local development.

output_examples.input: I need a Python app to authenticate to Azure Blob Storage locally and in App Service.
output: Use DefaultAzureCredential for one code path across both environments.
Assign the App Service identity the required storage role.
Use Azure CLI login for local development instead of storing secrets.

output_examples.input: My CI job needs Azure authentication for a Python deployment script.
output: Use a service principal only when managed identity or workload identity is unavailable.
Store tenant, client, and secret values in the CI secret store.
Limit the service principal to the minimum Azure roles needed.

output_examples.input: I need to get an access token for Azure Resource Manager.
output_examples.output: The response explains the correct Azure resource scope, token lifetime handling, and why tokens should not be logged.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential for code that runs in both local and Azure environments.
Use managed identity in production whenever the workload runs on Azure infrastructure.
Keep secrets in environment or secret-management systems and avoid logging tokens.
anti_patterns: Hardcoding tenant IDs, client secrets, or access tokens in source files.
Granting broad Azure roles when a narrower scope is enough.
Leaving interactive browser authentication enabled for unattended production jobs.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps generate and review Python authentication patterns using the Azure Identity SDK.

faq.question: Does it create Azure credentials for me?
faq.answer: No. It explains code patterns and configuration, but users must create identities and secrets in Azure.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is DefaultAzureCredential always the right choice?
faq.answer: It is recommended for many scenarios, but some workloads need a specific credential type or custom chain.

faq.question: Does the skill access my Azure environment?
faq.answer: No evidence found that the skill itself accesses Azure. It contains Markdown examples for users to adapt.

faq.question: What security review is still needed?
faq.answer: Review identity permissions, secret storage, token logging, and deployment configuration before production use.
